morning.hook: You got the raise, the promotion, the apartment you wanted. For a few weeks, it was glorious. Then it became the new normal, and you started eyeing the next thing. That drift back to baseline has a name — and it quietly explains why achievement so rarely makes us as happy as we predicted.
explanation_paragraphs: In 1978, psychologists Philip Brickman, Dan Coates, and Ronnie Janoff-Bulman published a study comparing two groups you'd expect to sit at opposite ends of the happiness spectrum: people who had won large lotteries, and people who had become paraplegic after accidents. The result was startling. The lottery winners were not meaningfully happier than a control group, and reported taking less pleasure in everyday activities. The accident victims, while initially less happy, had adapted far more than anyone predicted. Within a surprisingly short time, both groups had drifted back toward something like their original level of well-being.

This is the hedonic treadmill, sometimes called hedonic adaptation: the well-documented tendency for people to return to a relatively stable baseline of happiness after positive or negative life events. A windfall lifts you up, but you adapt, and the elevation fades. A setback knocks you down, but you adapt to that too. Like walking on a treadmill, you keep moving — chasing the next gain — yet your subjective position stays roughly where it started. Brickman and Campbell had coined the term 'hedonic treadmill' a few years earlier, in a 1971 essay, and decades of research have refined, though not erased, the core finding.

The picture is not as bleak as the original framing suggested, and this nuance matters. Later research, notably by Ed Diener and colleagues, found that baselines are not perfectly fixed — they sit above neutral for most people, they differ from person to person, and some events (like prolonged unemployment or the loss of a spouse) can shift the baseline lastingly rather than just temporarily. Adaptation is also uneven: we habituate fast to material gains and status, but more slowly, if at all, to things like chronic noise, ongoing social connection, or a sense of meaning. The treadmill is real, but it is not a perfect circle.

The practical force of this is large. Most career and consumer striving runs on 'affective forecasting' — our prediction of how we'll feel once we get the thing — and the research consistently shows we overestimate both the intensity and the duration of the happiness a gain will bring. If you understand the treadmill, you stop expecting any single achievement to permanently raise your floor, and you redirect effort toward the things adaptation doesn't fully erase: relationships, autonomy, growth, meaning, and experiences over possessions. The goal isn't to stop wanting. It's to stop mistaking the next rung for the destination.
morning.why_today: The term 'hedonic treadmill' was coined by Brickman, P. & Campbell, D. T. (1971), 'Hedonic relativism and planning the good society.' The famous lottery-winners and accident-victims study is Brickman, P., Coates, D. & Janoff-Bulman, R. (1978), 'Lottery winners and accident victims: Is happiness relative?', Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, with major refinements by Ed Diener and colleagues (notably 'Beyond the Hedonic Treadmill,' American Psychologist, 2006).
morning.morning_challenge:
Recall a goal you achieved in the past few years that you were sure would make you durably happier — a job, a purchase, a milestone.

Honestly estimate: how long did the boost actually last before it became your new normal? Days? Weeks? Months?

Now name one source of well-being in your life that has NOT faded the same way — a relationship, a craft, a place, a routine. That contrast is your map to what the treadmill can't erase. Aim more of your effort there.

midday.reframe: The treadmill isn't telling you wanting is pointless. It's telling you that gains in status and stuff are leased, not owned — you adapt and they fade. So stop budgeting your effort as if the next achievement will permanently raise your floor. Spend it on the things adaptation is bad at erasing.
midday.itw_label: IN THE WILD — TYPE A · BRICKMAN, COATES & JANOFF-BULMAN, JPSP, 1978. DIENER ET AL., AMERICAN PSYCHOLOGIST, 2006.
itw_paragraphs: The original 1978 study is the canonical example: lottery winners, surveyed after their windfall, were not significantly happier than non-winners and reported deriving less pleasure from ordinary pleasures like eating breakfast or chatting with a friend — the big high appeared to recalibrate the small ones. Paraplegic accident victims, meanwhile, adapted far more than observers predicted. The asymmetry between how dramatic the events were and how modest the lasting happiness gap became is what launched fifty years of research.

Affective forecasting research by Daniel Gilbert and Timothy Wilson shows the treadmill at the moment of decision: people systematically overestimate how long both good and bad events will affect them — a bias they call 'impact bias.' Assistant professors predicted that getting or being denied tenure would shape their happiness for years; in reality the effect washed out far faster. The same bias inflates how good we expect a new car, raise, or gadget to make us feel, fueling purchases and pursuits that under-deliver on the predicted glow.

The refinements matter as much as the headline. Diener's work found that set points sit comfortably above neutral for most people, vary by individual, and can move: long-term unemployment and widowhood can lower the baseline durably rather than temporarily, while strong social relationships and a sense of purpose resist adaptation. This is why happiness research consistently nudges people toward spending on experiences and relationships rather than possessions — we habituate to the new couch far faster than to a regular ritual with people we love.
midday.quote: Lottery winners were not happier than controls and took significantly less pleasure from a series of mundane events.
midday.attribution: Brickman, Coates & Janoff-Bulman, 'Lottery Winners and Accident Victims' (1978)
midday.midday_nudge: Next time you catch yourself thinking 'I'll be happy once I get X,' add a quiet second clause: '...for about three weeks, then it'll be normal.' Then ask what you'd do differently if you fully believed that. Usually the answer points away from the rung and toward something slower-burning.

quiz.question: What did Brickman, Coates, and Janoff-Bulman's 1978 study of lottery winners and accident victims primarily demonstrate?
options: That money has no effect whatsoever on anyone's happiness
That people tend to drift back toward a baseline level of well-being after major positive or negative events, more than intuition predicts
That accident victims are inherently happier than lottery winners
That happiness is entirely determined by genetics
quiz.explanation: The study's power came from the asymmetry: two dramatically different life events produced far smaller and less lasting differences in happiness than anyone expected, because both groups adapted toward their prior baseline. It doesn't claim money is irrelevant or that the baseline is identical for everyone — just that adaptation pulls us back toward our own set point after big shocks in either direction.

quiz.question: How did later research by Diener and colleagues refine the original 'fixed treadmill' picture?
options: It proved the treadmill doesn't exist and people stay at peak happiness after gains
It showed baselines are perfectly identical across all people
It found baselines sit above neutral, vary by individual, and can shift lastingly from some events — so adaptation is real but not a perfect, uniform circle
It concluded that only negative events cause adaptation
quiz.explanation: Diener's 'Beyond the Hedonic Treadmill' work kept the core insight but added crucial nuance: set points differ between people, typically sit above neutral, and aren't immovable — events like long-term unemployment or losing a spouse can durably lower them. This matters because it means some pursuits genuinely move the baseline, while others just trigger temporary highs you adapt away.

quiz.question: Knowing about the hedonic treadmill and 'impact bias,' which strategy is best supported by the research for building durable well-being?
options: Stop pursuing any goals, since adaptation erases all gains equally
Pursue ever-larger material rewards so the highs outpace adaptation

Weight effort toward things adaptation is poor at erasing — relationships, autonomy, growth, meaning, and experiences — rather than expecting any single material gain to permanently lift your baseline
Make major purchases more frequently to maintain a constant stream of novelty
quiz.explanation: Because we adapt quickly to status and possessions but more slowly to social connection, purpose, and experiences, the evidence-based move is to redirect effort toward the adaptation-resistant sources. The point isn't to stop wanting or to chase bigger highs — that just speeds up the treadmill — but to stop mistaking the next acquisition for a permanent upgrade to your happiness floor.

closing: Every gain you're chasing will feel huge, then ordinary. That's not a flaw in you — it's the treadmill, and it never switches off. The freedom is in knowing it: stop waiting for the next achievement to fix your baseline, and pour your effort into the things that don't fade.

sources.label: Brickman, P. & Campbell, D. T. — 'Hedonic Relativism and Planning the Good Society', in Adaptation-Level Theory (1971)
sources.detail: The essay that coined the term 'hedonic treadmill.'

sources.label: Brickman, P., Coates, D. & Janoff-Bulman, R. — 'Lottery Winners and Accident Victims: Is Happiness Relative?', Journal of Personality and Social Psychology (1978)
sources.detail: The foundational study comparing the well-being of lottery winners and accident victims.

sources.label: Diener, E., Lucas, R. E. & Scollon, C. N. — 'Beyond the Hedonic Treadmill', American Psychologist (2006)
sources.detail: Major review revising the set-point theory, showing baselines vary, sit above neutral, and can shift with certain life events.

ai_prompt: I want to pressure-test a goal I'm chasing against the hedonic treadmill — I suspect I'm overestimating how durably it'll make me happier. Before analyzing, ask me what the goal is, how long I expect the happiness boost to last, and which past achievements faded faster than I predicted. Then help me redirect some effort toward adaptation-resistant sources. Here's the goal: [describe what you're chasing and the happiness you expect from it]
